facts.text: Ray Charles is often called the 'Father of Soul' for blending gospel, blues, and jazz in the 1950s.
Motown Records was founded by Berry Gordy Jr. in Detroit, Michigan in 1959.
Aretha Franklin earned the title 'Queen of Soul' and had over 100 singles on the Billboard charts.
The term 'rhythm and blues' was coined by Jerry Wexler in 1948 as a replacement for the term 'race music.'
Sam Cooke's 'A Change Is Gonna Come' became an anthem of the Civil Rights Movement after its release in 1964.
Stevie Wonder signed with Motown Records at age 11, earning the nickname 'Little Stevie Wonder.'
Marvin Gaye's album 'What's Going On' was initially rejected by Berry Gordy, who thought it was too political.
The Supremes had 12 number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100, more than any other American group at the time.
James Brown is known as the 'Godfather of Soul' and is credited with laying the groundwork for funk music.
Whitney Houston's debut album is the best-selling debut by a female artist, with over 25 million copies sold.
Otis Redding's '(Sittin' On) The Dock of the Bay' was released posthumously and became the first posthumous number-one single in the U.S.
Stax Records, based in Memphis, Tennessee, was a major rival to Motown and home to artists like Otis Redding and Isaac Hayes.
Al Green is considered one of the greatest soul singers of all time, known for hits like 'Let's Stay Together.'
Beyoncé's album 'Lemonade' debuted at number one and won a Peabody Award for its visual storytelling.
The Temptations were one of Motown's most successful acts, known for their choreography and harmonies.
Tina Turner's 'What's Love Got to Do with It' won three Grammy Awards in 1985, including Record of the Year.
Luther Vandross was known as the 'Velvet Voice' and sold over 40 million records worldwide.
Prince's 'Purple Rain' album spent 24 consecutive weeks at number one on the Billboard 200.
Usher's 'Confessions' album sold over 10 million copies in the United States alone.
Alicia Keys' debut album 'Songs in A Minor' sold over 236,000 copies in its first week.
The Four Tops' 'I Can't Help Myself (Sugar Pie Honey Bunch)' was the number-one song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1965.
Frank Ocean's 'Blonde' was released exclusively on Apple Music in 2016, bypassing his label Def Jam.
Gladys Knight & the Pips won the first-ever Grammy Award for Best R&B Vocal Performance by a Group in 1974.
SZA's debut studio album 'Ctrl' was certified platinum by the RIAA within a year of its release.
The Weeknd's 'Blinding Lights' became one of the longest-charting songs in Billboard Hot 100 history.
D'Angelo's 'Voodoo' is widely regarded as a landmark album in the neo-soul movement.
Erykah Badu is often called the 'Queen of Neo-Soul' for her debut album 'Baduizm' in 1997.
Aretha Franklin was the first woman inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1987.
Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' is the best-selling album of all time, with estimated sales of over 66 million copies.
Lauryn Hill's 'The Miseducation of Lauryn Hill' won five Grammy Awards, including Album of the Year.
Bobby Brown's 'Don't Be Cruel' album helped define the new jack swing era of the late 1980s.
The Isley Brothers' career spanned over 50 years, from doo-wop to funk to quiet storm R&B.
Teddy Pendergrass was the first African American male artist to have five consecutive platinum albums.
Chaka Khan's 'I Feel for You' features a rap intro by Meli'sa Morgan and a harmonica solo by Stevie Wonder.
Mary J. Blige is known as the 'Queen of Hip-Hop Soul' for blending R&B with hip-hop production.
Barry White's deep bass voice and lush orchestral arrangements made him a defining figure of 1970s soul.
TLC's 'CrazySexyCool' was the first album by a female group to be certified diamond by the RIAA.
Smokey Robinson was a songwriter, producer, and lead singer of the Miracles, one of Motown's first groups.
Boyz II Men's 'End of the Road' spent 13 weeks at number one, breaking Elvis Presley's record at the time.
Janet Jackson's 'Rhythm Nation 1814' produced seven Billboard Hot 100 top-five singles.
Pharrell Williams produced major R&B hits for artists like Usher, Justin Timberlake, and Kelis through The Neptunes.
The O'Jays' 'Love Train' was a number-one hit in 1973 and became one of the signature songs of Philadelphia soul.
Jill Scott's debut album 'Who Is Jill Scott? Words and Sounds Vol. 1' went platinum in 2000.
New Edition is often credited as a prototype for modern boy bands, blending R&B vocals with choreography.
Curtis Mayfield's 'Superfly' soundtrack is considered one of the greatest soundtracks ever recorded.
Destiny's Child sold over 60 million records worldwide, making them one of the best-selling girl groups of all time.
Isaac Hayes won an Academy Award for Best Original Song for the 'Shaft' theme in 1972.
Aaliyah's album 'One in a Million' introduced a futuristic sound to R&B, produced largely by Timbaland.
The Motown sound typically featured a driving rhythm section, tambourine, and orchestral strings.
R. Kelly's 'I Believe I Can Fly' won three Grammy Awards in 1998, including Best R&B Song.
Patti LaBelle's vocal range spans over three octaves, earning her the title 'Godmother of Soul.'
Childish Gambino's 'Awaken, My Love!' drew heavily from 1970s funk and soul, particularly Parliament-Funkadelic.
The term 'quiet storm' was coined after Smokey Robinson's 1975 album of the same name, describing a mellow R&B subgenre.
Ne-Yo wrote 'Let Me Love You' for Mario before launching his own successful singing career.
H.E.R. won the Grammy for Song of the Year in 2021 for 'I Can't Breathe.'
The Staple Singers blended gospel and soul, scoring a number-one hit with 'I'll Take You There' in 1972.
Musiq Soulchild's debut album 'Aijuswanaseing' went platinum and helped popularize neo-soul in the early 2000s.
Whitney Houston had seven consecutive number-one singles on the Billboard Hot 100, a record at the time.
Anderson .Paak's 'Malibu' blended R&B, soul, hip-hop, and funk, earning critical acclaim in 2016.
Stevie Wonder's 'Songs in the Key of Life' debuted at number one and won the Grammy for Album of the Year.
En Vogue's 'Free Your Mind' addressed racial prejudice and became a top-ten hit in 1992.
Babyface has written and produced over 26 number-one R&B hits for various artists.
Toni Braxton's 'Un-Break My Heart' spent 11 weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
Silk Sonic, the duo of Bruno Mars and Anderson .Paak, won four Grammy Awards for their debut album 'An Evening with Silk Sonic.'
Jackie Wilson's energetic stage performances in the 1950s and 1960s influenced artists from James Brown to Michael Jackson.
The Chi-Lites' 'Oh Girl' was the first number-one pop hit for the Brunswick Records label in 1972.
Brandy and Monica's 'The Boy Is Mine' became one of the longest-running number-one duets in Billboard history.
Tank is known as the 'R&B General' and has written songs for artists like Aaliyah, Chris Brown, and Jamie Foxx.
Anita Baker's 'Rapture' won two Grammy Awards and has been certified five times platinum.
The Drifters' 'There Goes My Baby' was one of the first R&B songs to feature orchestral strings, recorded in 1959.
Janelle Monáe's albums form a sci-fi narrative called the 'Metropolis' saga, blending R&B with afrofuturism.
Bill Withers wrote and performed 'Lean on Me,' 'Ain't No Sunshine,' and 'Just the Two of Us,' all of which became standards.
Chris Brown's self-titled debut album was certified double platinum, launched when he was just 16 years old.
Atlantic Records was a pioneering label for R&B and soul, signing artists like Ray Charles and Aretha Franklin.
Donny Hathaway's live album, recorded at the Bitter End in New York, is considered one of the greatest live R&B albums.
Jhené Aiko's 'Souled Out' debuted at number three on the Billboard 200 in 2014.
The Gap Band blended funk, R&B, and soul, scoring hits like 'Outstanding' and 'You Dropped a Bomb on Me' in the early 1980s.
Marvin Gaye's 'Sexual Healing' was his final major hit and won two Grammy Awards in 1983.
Khalid's debut album 'American Teen' was certified triple platinum and earned five Grammy nominations.
The Pointer Sisters crossed over from R&B to pop with hits like 'Jump (For My Love)' and 'I'm So Excited.'
Philadelphia International Records, founded by Kenny Gamble and Leon Huff, pioneered the Philadelphia soul sound.
Tinashe's '2 On' became a top-25 Billboard hit and helped establish her as a major R&B artist in 2014.
Sam & Dave's 'Soul Man' became one of the most iconic soul songs of the 1960s and won a Grammy Award.
Solange's 'A Seat at the Table' debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 in 2016.
Gerald Levert, son of O'Jays member Eddie Levert, became a successful solo R&B artist in the 1990s.
Normani was a member of Fifth Harmony before launching her solo R&B career with 'Motivation' in 2019.
The Commodores, featuring Lionel Richie, blended funk and soul ballads, producing hits like 'Easy' and 'Three Times a Lady.'
Missy Elliott's innovative music videos and production style redefined R&B and hip-hop in the late 1990s.
Earth, Wind & Fire's 'September' is one of the most-streamed R&B/soul songs of all time on digital platforms.
Daniel Caesar's 'Get You' featuring Kali Uchis became a sleeper hit and R&B standard.
The concept of 'blue-eyed soul' refers to white artists who perform soul and R&B music, such as Hall & Oates and Teena Marie.
Ciara's debut single 'Goodies' spent seven weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 2004.
Sade's smooth blend of jazz, soul, and soft pop earned her the nickname 'the Quiet Storm Queen.'
Joe's 'I Wanna Know' was nominated for a Grammy and became one of the biggest R&B ballads of 2000.
The Manhattans' 'Kiss and Say Goodbye' was the first certified platinum single by an R&B group.
Jazmine Sullivan's 'Heaux Tales' EP won a Grammy Award for Best R&B Album in 2022.
Keith Sweat is considered a pioneer of new jack swing, with his 1987 debut 'Make It Last Forever.'
Ella Mai's 'Boo'd Up' was the longest-running number-one hit on the Billboard R&B chart in 2018.
The Spinners had five top-ten pop hits in the 1970s, including 'I'll Be Around' and 'Could It Be I'm Falling in Love.'
Summer Walker's 'Over It' debuted at number two on the Billboard 200, the highest-charting debut by an R&B female artist at the time.
Mariah Carey holds the record for the most number-one singles by a solo artist on the Billboard Hot 100 with 19.
The Funk Brothers were the uncredited studio band behind most of Motown's hits in the 1960s and 1970s.
Bryson Tiller's debut album 'Trapsoul' popularized the 'trapsoul' subgenre, blending trap production with R&B vocals.
Roberta Flack's 'Killing Me Softly with His Song' won the Grammy for Record of the Year in 1974.
The Weeknd's real name is Abel Tesfaye, and he grew up in Scarborough, Ontario, Canada.
Harold Melvin & the Blue Notes featured Teddy Pendergrass as lead vocalist on the classic 'If You Don't Know Me by Now.'
Kehlani's 'SweetSexySavage' debuted in the top five of the Billboard 200 in 2017.
The 'Chitlin' Circuit' was a network of performance venues across the U.S. that were safe for African American musicians during segregation.
Maxwell's 'BLACKsummers'night' debuted at number one on the Billboard 200 in 2009.
Dionne Warwick had over 30 hit singles produced by Burt Bacharach and Hal David during the 1960s.
Giveon's baritone voice drew comparisons to classic soul singers, and his feature on Drake's 'Chicago Freestyle' launched his career.
The term 'neo-soul' was popularized by music journalist and Motown executive Kedar Massenburg in the late 1990s.
Tammi Terrell and Marvin Gaye recorded iconic duets including 'Ain't No Mountain High Enough' and 'You're All I Need to Get By.'
6LACK's debut mixtape 'Free 6LACK' was certified gold and featured the hit 'PRBLMS.'
The Delfonics' falsetto-driven sound influenced the Philadelphia soul movement of the early 1970s.
Trey Songz's 'Can't Help but Wait' earned him his first Grammy nomination in 2008.
Stevie Wonder played harmonica, piano, and drums by the age of nine.
Keyshia Cole's 'The Way It Is' went platinum and established her as a major R&B voice in 2005.
The Bar-Kays were the house band at Stax Records and tragically lost several members in the same plane crash that killed Otis Redding.
Miguel's 'Adorn' won the Grammy for Best R&B Song in 2013.
Tevin Campbell's performance of 'Round and Round' in the film 'Graffiti Bridge' launched his career at age 14.
Doja Cat's 'Say So' blended R&B, pop, and disco influences and reached number one thanks in part to a TikTok dance trend.
The Whispers had a career spanning four decades, known for hits like 'Rock Steady' and 'And the Beat Goes On.'
Lucky Daye's 'Painted' was nominated for four Grammy Awards in 2020.
Diana Ross left The Supremes in 1970 to pursue a solo career that included hits like 'I'm Coming Out' and 'Upside Down.'
Snoh Aalegra's 'I Want You Around' became a major streaming hit and introduced her to a wider audience.
The Sound of Philadelphia, characterized by lush orchestrations and smooth vocals, was pioneered by producers Gamble and Huff.
112's 'Peaches & Cream' and 'Cupid' were defining hits of early-2000s R&B.
Nina Simone blended classical piano training with jazz, blues, and soul, becoming known as the 'High Priestess of Soul.'
Brent Faiyaz's independent release 'Wasteland' debuted at number two on the Billboard 200 in 2022.
Rick James' 'Super Freak' became one of the most sampled songs in music history, notably used in MC Hammer's 'U Can't Touch This.'
Chloe x Halle were discovered by Beyoncé after posting YouTube covers and were signed to her Parkwood Entertainment label.
The Soul Stirrers, featuring a young Sam Cooke, were one of the first gospel groups to cross over into secular soul music.
Ari Lennox's 'Shea Butter Baby' was the first album released on J. Cole's Dreamville Records by a female artist.
Wilson Pickett's 'In the Midnight Hour' was co-written with guitarist Steve Cropper at Stax Records.
SWV's 'Weak' spent two weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1993.
The concept of 'melisma'—singing multiple notes on a single syllable—is a defining vocal technique in R&B and soul music.
PJ Morton, keyboardist of Maroon 5, has also won Grammy Awards for his solo R&B work.
Smokey Robinson wrote 'My Girl' for The Temptations, which became their first number-one single in 1965.
Victoria Monét won the Grammy for Best New Artist in 2024.
Solomon Burke is sometimes called the 'King of Rock and Soul' and was one of the founding fathers of soul music.
Xscape reunited in 2017 after a 19-year hiatus, with members Tiny Harris, Kandi Burruss, LaTocha Scott, and Tameka 'Tiny' Cottle.
The Miracles' 'Shop Around' was Motown's first million-selling single in 1960.
Ty Dolla $ign has written and featured on numerous R&B and pop hits, collaborating with artists across genres.
Ruth Brown was known as 'Miss Rhythm' and helped establish Atlantic Records as a major R&B label in the 1950s.
Jon Batiste's 'We Are' won the Grammy for Album of the Year in 2022, blending R&B, soul, and jazz.
The Detroit sound of Motown was characterized by a bass-heavy mix, tight arrangements, and pop-friendly melodies.
Sevyn Streeter co-wrote Chris Brown's 'Fine China' and Ariana Grande's 'The Way' before her solo career.
Teddy Riley is credited with creating the new jack swing genre, blending R&B vocals with hip-hop beats.
Maze featuring Frankie Beverly has been a staple of Black American culture, particularly at family reunions and cookouts.
Jessie Reyez's raw vocal style and confessional lyrics have earned comparisons to Amy Winehouse and Lauryn Hill.
The Jones Girls, proteges of Leon Huff, contributed to the Philadelphia International Records sound in the late 1970s.
Aretha Franklin's 'Respect' was originally written and recorded by Otis Redding in 1965.
Jeremih's 'Birthday Sex' reached number four on the Billboard Hot 100 in 2009.
Minnie Riperton was known for her five-octave vocal range, showcased in her hit 'Lovin' You.'
PARTYNEXTDOOR co-wrote Rihanna's 'Work,' which spent nine weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
Ray Charles' 'I Got a Woman' is often cited as one of the first true soul songs, released in 1954.
Tiara Thomas co-wrote Wale's 'Bad' and later won a Grammy for co-writing Jazmine Sullivan's 'Pick Up Your Feelings.'
The Stylistics' falsetto-led ballads like 'You Make Me Feel Brand New' defined early 1970s romantic soul.
Gallant's falsetto-driven style has drawn comparisons to Prince and Maxwell.
Lloyd's 'You' featuring Lil Wayne was a crossover R&B-hip-hop hit in 2007.
The Apollo Theater in Harlem has served as a launching pad for R&B and soul artists since the 1930s.
Ashanti's self-titled debut album sold over 504,000 copies in its first week in 2002, setting a SoundScan record for a female debut.
Teddy Pendergrass' 'women-only' concerts in the 1970s were some of the first of their kind.
The Emotions' 'Best of My Love' was produced by Earth, Wind & Fire's Maurice White and reached number one in 1977.
dvsn is a Canadian R&B duo signed to Drake's OVO Sound label.
Sam Cooke founded SAR Records, one of the first record labels owned by an African American artist.
Mario's 'Let Me Love You' spent nine weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 2005.
Teena Marie, known as 'Lady T,' was one of the most prominent white artists in R&B, mentored by Rick James.
Sabrina Claudio's 'Belong to You' went viral and helped popularize bedroom R&B on streaming platforms.
The Platters were one of the first R&B groups to cross over into the pop mainstream in the 1950s with 'The Great Pretender.'
Omarion was the lead singer of B2K before launching a solo career with the hit 'Ice Box' featuring Timbaland.
Bilal's avant-garde approach to R&B has earned him critical acclaim and collaborations with artists like Kendrick Lamar and Common.
Atlantic Starr's 'Always' was a number-one hit in 1987 and became a popular wedding song.
Meshell Ndegeocello's 'Plantation Lullabies' blended funk, soul, and hip-hop, earning two Grammy nominations in 1994.
The Weeknd's 'After Hours' album debuted at number one in over 30 countries.

Quincy Jones produced Michael Jackson's 'Off the Wall,' 'Thriller,' and 'Bad,' three of the best-selling albums ever.

The Soul Train television show, created by Don Cornelius, ran from 1971 to 2006 and showcased R&B and soul artists.
Tamia's 'So Into You' is considered one of the quintessential R&B ballads of the late 1990s.
Al Jarreau was the only vocalist to win Grammy Awards in three separate categories: jazz, pop, and R&B.
Raphael Saadiq's 'Stone Rollin'' album paid homage to classic Motown and soul sounds.
Aaliyah died in a plane crash in the Bahamas at age 22, cutting short one of R&B's most promising careers.
LeAnn Rimes' crossover success helped bridge country and R&B audiences in the late 1990s.
The Bee Gees wrote 'Islands in the Stream' for Kenny Rogers and Dolly Parton but originally intended it as an R&B song.
Ella Fitzgerald, while primarily a jazz singer, influenced countless R&B vocalists with her impeccable phrasing and technique.
Jacquees declared himself the 'King of R&B' on social media in 2018, sparking widespread debate.
The Clark Sisters are considered the most successful gospel group in history and have heavily influenced R&B vocal styles.
Ginuwine's 'Pony' was produced by Timbaland and became one of the most iconic R&B songs of the 1990s.

James Brown's 'Say It Loud – I'm Black and I'm Proud' became an anthem for the Black pride movement in 1968.
Carl Thomas' 'I Wish' was one of the biggest R&B ballads of 2000.
The concept of 'call and response,' rooted in African musical traditions, is a fundamental element of soul music.
Muni Long's 'Hrs and Hrs' went viral on social media and earned a Grammy nomination for Best R&B Song.

The Supremes' Diana Ross was replaced by Jean Terrell when Ross departed for a solo career in 1970.

The Manhattans featured Gerald Alston as lead singer on their biggest hit 'Shining Star' in 1980.

Otis Redding died in a plane crash on December 10, 1967, at the age of 26.
Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis produced 16 number-one pop hits, mostly for Janet Jackson and other R&B artists.

Monica's 'Angel of Mine' spent four weeks at number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1999.
The Intruders' 'I'll Always Love My Mama' was one of the earliest Philadelphia International Records hits.

Tyrese Gibson transitioned from R&B singing to acting, appearing in the Fast & Furious franchise.

Tinashe independently released several mixtapes before signing with RCA Records for her debut album 'Aquarius.'

Summer Walker's debut album 'Over It' broke Apple Music's first-week streaming record for an R&B album by a female artist.

Lalah Hathaway, daughter of Donny Hathaway, is known for her ability to sing polyphonic overtones, producing two notes simultaneously.

Peabo Bryson recorded popular duets with Roberta Flack, including 'Tonight, I Celebrate My Love.'

Keyshia Cole's reality TV show 'Keyshia Cole: The Way It Is' ran for four seasons on BET.

Janet Jackson's 'Control' album represented her declaration of independence from her family's management.
Tank's 'When We' from his album 'Sex, Love & Pain' is considered one of the best R&B songs of the 2000s.
The S.O.S. Band's 'Just Be Good to Me' was produced by Jimmy Jam and Terry Lewis.

The Coasters' humorous story-songs like 'Yakety Yak' and 'Charlie Brown' helped bridge R&B and rock and roll.
Alex Isley, daughter of Ernie Isley, has carried on the family's R&B legacy with critically acclaimed releases.

Jade's 'Don't Walk Away' reached the top five on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1993.
The term 'Southern soul' describes a grittier, more blues-influenced subgenre of soul from the American South.
The-Dream has written or co-written songs for Beyoncé, Rihanna, Mariah Carey, and Justin Bieber.
Amber Mark's 'Three Dimensions Deep' blended R&B with pop, dance, and bossa nova influences.
The Harlem Renaissance of the 1920s and 1930s laid cultural groundwork for the development of R&B and soul music.
Ginuwine, Timbaland, and Missy Elliott formed a creative trio that produced some of the most innovative R&B of the late 1990s.
Corinne Bailey Rae's self-titled debut album featured 'Put Your Records On,' which reached the top 30 in the U.S.
